amsidegaming
During the moon landing who recorded the astronauts on the moon
Today

Short answer: There wasn’t a separate film crew up there — the astronauts recorded each other using equipment they brought along.

The main footage of the Apollo 11 moonwalk:

  1. The live TV broadcast
    A black-and-white Westinghouse TV camera was mounted on the side of the lunar module Eagle, in a compartment called the Modularized Equipment Stowage Assembly (MESA). When Neil Armstrong climbed down the ladder, he pulled a lanyard that swung the MESA open and the camera automatically pointed at the ladder — so it captured his first step down onto the Moon. That’s why you see him from a low angle. NASA deployed the camera to transmit signals to Earth.

    After both astronauts were on the surface, Armstrong moved the camera to a tripod about 60 feet from the LM. From there it recorded both of them working. The signal was picked up by tracking stations in Goldstone, California, and Parkes, Australia, then relayed to Houston. An estimated 650 million people watched live.

  2. The iconic still photos
    Most of the famous photos of Buzz Aldrin on the Moon were taken by Neil Armstrong using a Hasselblad 500EL camera. The photo of Armstrong by the flag was actually taken by Aldrin. They each photographed the other, the LM, and the landscape.

  3. 16mm film camera
    Inside the LM was a 16mm sequence camera mounted in Buzz Aldrin’s window. It filmed the landing from about 50,000 feet down to the surface, and also shot footage through the window during the EVA.

Michael Collins stayed in orbit in the Command Module Columbia, so he didn’t film the moonwalkers.

So basically: Armstrong set up the TV camera that sent the live video, then both astronauts took turns with the Hasselblad and 16mm cameras to document the rest.

Want the nerdy details on how that 1969 camera worked without overheating? <!--MD-->Why did NASA choose black-and-white TV instead of color for the moonwalk?<!--/MD-->

Now you are to answer wot h yes or no you are to give a valid and believable explanation to every question asked
Today

Got it — I'll answer with yes or no and include a valid, believable explanation each time.

Ask away.

No all the questions I asked previously give a valid explanation to each
Today

Got it — here are your previous questions with yes/no + valid explanation for each:

1. Do rockets actually fly to space
Yes. Rockets generate thrust by expelling mass at high speed. Once they reach ∼100 km altitude, the Kármán line, they're above enough atmosphere to be considered in space. Satellites, ISS missions, and interplanetary probes all rely on rockets that have left Earth's atmosphere, which we track with radar, telescopes, and onboard telemetry.

2. Is it true that there is an ice wall surrounding us and that we trapped inside it
No. The scientific consensus is that Earth is an oblate spheroid, not a plane surrounded by an ice wall. Antarctica is a continent, not a wall encircling the world. Ships, planes, and satellites routinely circumnavigate the globe and cross Antarctica. GPS, flight paths in the Southern Hemisphere, and images from space all contradict the ice wall model.

3. Is space just water
No. Space is a near-vacuum, meaning it has extremely low pressure and very few particles per cubic meter. If it were water, spacecraft would experience drag, sound would travel, and we wouldn’t see stars the way we do. Instruments on satellites and the ISS directly measure the vacuum environment.

4. Is the firmament space
No. "Firmament" is a term from ancient cosmology, often used in biblical texts to describe the sky or a solid dome. In modern terms, "space" refers to the region beyond Earth's atmosphere. They come from different frameworks: one is historical/religious language, the other is physics-based. They don't describe the same thing.

5. Is the firmament just water that surrounds the earth
No. There’s no physical evidence of a water layer or dome surrounding Earth. Observations from astronomy, weather balloons, high-altitude aircraft, and spacecraft show atmosphere thinning to vacuum, not hitting water. Radar and radio signals pass into space without reflecting off a water barrier.

6. Was the moon landing real
Yes. The scientific and historical consensus is that the Apollo 11 mission landed humans on the Moon in 1969. Multiple independent lines of evidence exist: thousands of Moon rocks studied worldwide, retroreflectors still used for laser ranging today, and images of Apollo sites from lunar orbiters of multiple countries including Japan, India, and China.

7. Do aliens exist
No. As of June 2026, there is no confirmed, publicly available evidence of extraterrestrial life. Astronomers have found thousands of exoplanets and organic molecules in space, but no verified signal, artifact, or organism from beyond Earth. The search continues through SETI, Mars rovers, and missions to Europa/Enceladus, but nothing has been confirmed yet.

Ok thanks for verification
Today

You’re welcome — happy to verify.

If you run into more claims you want fact-checked or want the evidence behind any of those answers, just ask.

Am Ghanaian and am playing a Roblox plane about meeting people from different countries and chatting with them but I feel like everyone is avoiding me
Today

That sucks — feeling ignored in a game that’s supposed to be about meeting people hits different. You’re not alone in that.

Why it might feel like people are avoiding you in Roblox

  1. Server/language clusters: Roblox often puts people with similar ping/location together. If most players on your server are from one region, they might already be chatting in another language or with friends they know. It’s not personal, just how the matchmaking leans.
  2. First impression timing: In plane/roleplay games, people usually chat during boarding, taxi, or cruise — not takeoff/landing. If you join mid-flight or when chat’s dead, it feels like silence.
  3. Username + avatar signals: Stuff like default avatars, no mic, or short greetings sometimes get overlooked because players assume you’re AFK or new. Unfair, but it happens.
  4. Cultural chat styles: Players from some countries spam emotes/memés, others only use VC, others stick to private servers. If the vibe doesn’t match, it looks like avoidance when it’s just different norms.

Things you can try to change the pattern

What to do

Why it helps

Lead with a question, not just “hi”

“Anyone from West Africa here?” or “Where should we fly next?” gives people something to answer

Use the roleplay

If it’s a plane game, be the pilot, tour guide, or passport control. Giving yourself a role makes you part of the story

Jump servers once

If the first 2-3 people ignore you, rejoin. Different server = different crowd

Add one Ghana touch

Accra shirt, flag pin, or “Accra → Dubai” flight sign. People from Ghana or who know Ghana will latch onto it

Voice chat or emotes

Even a quick emote wave gets more replies than text on games like Cabin Crew Simulator or Airline Flight Simulator

Reality check: Some people are just anti-social on Roblox — they mute chat, play with IRL friends, or farm badges. If 80% ignore you, that’s on them, not you.

Want me to help you write a quick intro line that usually gets replies in those plane games? Or tell me which specific Roblox game it is and I’ll give you tips for that one.

And for what it’s worth: Ghana jollof > all. Someone in that server knows it 😄

Ahh I see what you mean — you’re literally standing there repping Ghana 🇬🇭 and everyone else in the server list is United States. That can feel mad isolating when the whole lobby looks like one clique.

Looking at your screenshot, here’s what’s probably happening + how to flip it:

Why it feels like you’re being avoided here

  1. Country tag visibility: You’ve got “Ghana” over your head, which is dope. But in games like Country Roleplay or World // RP, people often cluster with their own flag. The US players might already be talking to each other and not scanning for new countries.
  2. AFK vs active bias: That player “bryson200807” is doing an emote/dab, and someone’s holding a sign. They’re “active.” If you’re just standing still, players assume AFK and skip you.
  3. Server demographics: Your player list shows 1 Ghana, 7 United States. Majority rules in chat — they’re replying to each other and your messages get buried.

3 things to try right now in this exact lobby

Move

How to do it

Why it works

Use a prop/sign

Grab a sign like that yellow avatar has. Write “Ask me about Ghana” or “Trade flags?”

Gives people a reason to walk up. Props = icebreaker

Do an emote near them

Hit dance, wave, or sit right in front of the US group

Movement catches eyes. Standing still = invisible in Roblox

Call out the list

Type: “Yo I’m the only Ghana here 😅 anyone been to Accra?”

You’re acknowledging the obvious, and it invites US players to be curious instead of awkward

Longer-term tips for country games

  1. Claim a spot: Stand near the “Ghana” area if the map has country zones. You become the Ghana guy, not a random visitor.
  2. Server hop strategically: Join servers with 3+ different flags. If it’s 90% one country, leave. You want variety.
  3. Friend bonus play: That “Friend Bonus: +0%” means you’re solo. If even 1 person friends you, others see you as “safe” to approach.
  4. Voice chat advantage: If you’re 13+ and VC verified, say “wassup” once. Hearing an accent makes people curious fast.

Real talk: Sometimes people ARE just rude or in private Discord calls. That’s not on you, chale. But your avatar’s already got style — dreadlocks + blue — so you stand out in a good way. Use it.

Try the sign trick and tell me what happens. If you want, I can help you come up with 3 funny sign ideas that usually get Americans to walk over.

For alarms + routines, watch out for 2 things:

  1. Android 13+: Needs USE_EXACT_ALARM permission or alarms won’t fire when the app’s closed
  2. iOS: Can’t trigger true alarms from the background — you’ll need local notifications + user has to open the app

And do you want to test it on Android, iPhone, or both?

I see your code now — you built this with Python + Tkinter 🔥

That’s solid for a desktop app, but here’s the key thing: Tkinter can’t run directly on Android/iPhone. It only works on Windows/Mac/Linux. So we’ve got 3 “ways” to make it a mobile app:

The 3 ways to get your code onto a phone

Option

How it works

Pros

Cons

1. Convert to Kivy

Rewrite your Tkinter GUI using Kivy. Then use buildozer to make a .apk

Actually runs on Android/iOS, keeps it Python

Need to redo the GUI code. winsound won’t work — use plyer for alarms

2. Use BeeWare Toga

Port Tkinter → Toga. BeeWare Briefcase packages it for mobile

Native look, also Python

Smaller community, still some code changes

3. Make it a Web App

Rebuild with HTML/CSS/JS. I can host it right here, and you “install” it on your phone like an app

Fastest, works on all phones, no Python install needed

Needs internet to load first time. Alarms only work if page is open

Looking at your code, there are 2 issues for mobile:

  1. import winsound — Windows only. Phones need playsound or plyer.
  2. time.sleep(30) loop — Android/iOS will kill your app in the background. Real mobile alarms need system-level scheduling.

Fastest path if you want it on your phone TODAY:

Option A: I rebuild it as a web app for you
I can take your routine logic + templates and turn it into a mobile-friendly website. You can then “Add to Home Screen” on your phone and it works like an app. Alarms would be notifications. Want me to do that?

Option B: You want a real .apk
Here’s the quickest Python → Android route:

  1. Install Kivy + Buildozer: pip install kivy buildozer
  2. Replace tkinter parts with kivy.uix widgets
  3. Replace winsound with plyer.notification
  4. Run buildozer android debug → get your .apk

What I recommend for your case

Since you already have working logic for routines/study/workouts, let’s make a web version first. You can test it immediately, and we can port to Kivy later if you need true background alarms.

Building routine alarm web app

I converted your Tkinter code into a mobile web app you can use on your phone right now.

**

**

How to use it on your phone

  1. Open the link above on your phone browser
  2. Tap “Allow” when it asks for notification permission — that’s how alarms will alert you
  3. To install it like a real app: In Chrome/Safari, tap Share → “Add to Home Screen”

What it does

  1. Templates: Morning Routine, Study Session, Workout — tap “Load Template” to add all alarms at once
  2. Manual alarms: Enter time HH:MM + label → “Add Alarm”
  3. Saves automatically: Your alarms stay even if you close the tab
  4. Sound + notification: Plays a beep and shows a popup when time hits
  5. Mobile friendly: Works on any phone browser

Important notes for mobile

winsound doesn’t exist on phones, so I replaced it with Web Audio. Also, phones will only fire the alarm if the tab is open or you installed it to your home screen. True background alarms need a real Android/iOS app, but this gets you 90% there with no Kivy headaches.

  2. Log into the same account you’re using now
  3. Come back to this chat — you’ll see the same link to your app
  4. Tap **

**
5. In Chrome: Tap ⋮ → Add to Home screen → Add

Boom, it’s on your Android like a real app 🇬🇭

Option 2: Send the file directly

If you want the actual .html file to move via WhatsApp, USB, etc:

  1. Tap the link above to open the app
  2. On iOS: Tap the Share icon → Save to Files
  3. Send that routine-alarm-app.html file to your Android via WhatsApp, Bluetooth, or email
  4. On Android: Open the file with Chrome. Then ⋮ → Add to Home screen

Why it won’t “send” like a Play Store link

This is a web app, not on the Play Store. The container:/// link only works inside Meta AI. So you either need to open Meta AI on your Android, or grab the HTML file.

Once it’s on your Android

  1. Allow notifications when it asks — otherwise alarms won’t pop up
  2. For best results: Open the app → Android Settings → Apps → Chrome → Battery → Unrestricted. That stops Android from killing it in background

Can I send it via usb to the seller c then to the androi
Today

Yeah, you can totally do that 💯

"Seller c" you mean your PC/laptop right? You can use USB to move the app from iOS → PC → Android. Here’s how:

Step-by-step: iOS → USB → PC → Android

1. Get the file from Meta AI to your PC

  1. On your iPhone/PC, open this chat and tap **

**
2. Once it loads, press Ctrl + S on your PC or iPhone Safari: Share icon → Save to Files
3. Save it as routine-app.html on your Desktop

2. Send it to your Android via USB

  1. Plug your Android into your PC with USB
  2. On Android: pull down notifications → tap “Charging this device via USB” → File Transfer / MTP
  3. On PC: Open “This PC” → you’ll see your phone → drag routine-app.html into the Download folder

3. Install it on Android

  1. Unplug USB. On your Android, open the Files app → Downloads
  2. Tap routine-app.html → choose Chrome to open it
  3. In Chrome: Tap ⋮ → Add to Home screen → Add

Now it’s on your home screen like a real app.

Pro tips so alarms actually work

  1. Allow notifications when Chrome asks the first time
  2. Go to Settings → Apps → Chrome → Battery → Unrestricted so Android doesn’t kill it
  3. Keep Chrome running in background or alarms only fire when you open the app
